questions.text: I do not usually sense when someone is seeking reassurance rather than advice.
I notice that my sleep is rarely disrupted by emotional events from the day.
I find that my analytical temperament during stressful events has allowed me to serve as a stabilizing influence in environments where emotional reactions tend to escalate.
I prefer to ask for forgiveness rather than permission.
I tend to miss when a person mentions something they are proud of in a way designed not to sound like bragging.
I experience the anxiety of waiting as a physical pressure that builds in my chest.
I find that acting in my own interest when I could be helping others feels like a failure.
I tend to think of my time as my most valuable personal asset.
My internal emotional weather is rarely calm — there is almost always a storm or a brilliant sun.
I rarely detect when a person's interest in a conversation suddenly spikes because of something specific that was said.
I find it natural to follow well-given instructions and coordinate with peers.
I notice when a person brings up old information at a strategic moment to shift the dynamics of a conversation.
I notice when someone strategically drops a name or credential into conversation.
I believe that the quality of my personal environment directly affects the quality of my thinking.
I feel that delayed gratification is a skill worth cultivating.
I believe that decisions about how to respond to suffering should be grounded in systematic analysis rather than in the emotional character of the moment.
I experience the arrival of strong emotion as a wave that crests before I can brace for it.
I maintain consistent sleep and wake times because I value the stability they provide.
I hold the view that composure in the face of painful realities is a professional discipline that I owe to the people who count on me.
I let Monday morning unfold and see what the week brings.
I view deferring to others as a service to the group.
I would rather be the one steering than the one rowing.
I am comfortable overruling a group decision when I believe I am right.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the force that prevents me from settling into a life focused entirely on my own comfort and success.
I front-load effort on projects so the final stages are smoother.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering functions as a compass that reliably points me toward the most ethically important actions.
I believe that my capacity to recognize suffering as important is more central to who I am than my skills or achievements.
I experience embarrassing moments as brief flickers rather than searing burns.
I believe that every hour I spend on myself is an hour I could have spent helping someone.
I hold the view that my long-term well-being depends on consistently prioritizing my own needs.
I value variety in my daily routine because it keeps me mentally sharp and engaged.
I am aware of the way seating arrangements reflect social dynamics.
I implement new approaches fully and adjust based on live results.
I would rather be an integral part of a large group than lead a small one.
I tend to plan surprises and gestures for the people around me more than for myself.
I find that I adapt to new environments without much emotional upheaval.
